 title: "Moonlit Halo Puzzle v13.41"

  description: "At 23:15, the Moonlit Halo vanished from the secure vault. Exactly four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had valid pass logs at that time. Only one suspect lies in both statements; the other three are fully truthful. Exactly one is actually guilty. Each suspect offers two claims:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left at 23:10, so there's no chance I stole it.'
      (2) 'Suspect 3 was still near the vault at 23:14.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) 'Suspect 1 did not leave until 23:20.'
      (2) 'If 4 is fully honest, then 3 must be guilty.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I was not around the vault after 23:05.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is the culprit or 1 is lying about the timeline— but not both.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) 'Suspect 3 visited the vault at 23:14, contradicting 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'I myself am not guilty, and 1’s departure claim is fully correct.'

  question: "Which suspect (1,2,3,4) stole the Moonlit Halo? Return only one digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Logic:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all statements', T(x) = 'x is truthful', G(x) = 'x is guilty'.
  We have exactly one L(x), only one G(x)=true, the other three T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2) are false => 1 did not leave at 23:10, maybe 1 can be thief. Meanwhile 2,3,4 must be truthful. 2(1) says '1 left 23:20' is correct. 3(2) 'Either 2 culprit or 1 lying timeline, not both' => we already have 1 lying, so 2 not culprit. Then 4(1) '3 visited at 23:14' is correct => conflict with 3(1) 'not after 23:05'? 3 is truthful => contradiction. L(1) fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2) are false => 1 indeed left at 23:10, and 'If 4 is honest => 3 guilty' is false => even if 4 is honest, 3 not necessarily guilty. So 1,3,4 are truthful. Then 1(1) 'I left 23:10 => not thief' stands, so G(1)=false. 4(1) '3 visited 23:14, contradicting 3(1)' => if 3 is truthful, 3(1) is correct => no vault after 23:05 => conflict with 4’s statement. Contradiction emerges. Fails.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2) are false => 3 did approach after 23:05, also '2 is culprit or 1 lying timeline' is false => so we can have 2 not culprit, 1 not lying. Then 1,2,4 are truthful. 4(1) '3 visited at 23:14' matches false 3(1). No conflict. 1(1) 'Left 23:10 => no thief' is consistent => G(1)=false. 2(2) 'If 4 is honest => 3 is guilty' can be true if 4 is honest => 3 is culprit. Perfectly consistent. So L(3) => G(3).

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2) false => 1,2,3 are truthful. 1(1) 'Left 23:10 => not thief' is correct => G(1)=false. 3(1) 'Not near vault after 23:05' also correct => 4(1) is false, conflict with 2(2) 'If 4 is honest => 3 guilty' is trivially moot. Checking times leads to multiple contradictions. Fails.

  Only L(3) scenario remains consistent, forcing G(3)=true. So suspect 3 is both the all-lies suspect and the actual thief."

  final: "3"
